theorems.title: Finite version-space exhaustion bound
theorems.section: 2. Exhausting a Version Space
theorems.statementHtml:
If the hypothesis space \(H\) is finite, its cardinality denoted by \(|H|\), and \(Q\) is a sequence of \(m\geq 1\) independent random examples (chosen according to any fixed probability distribution on the instance space) of any target concept \(c\), then for any \(0<\epsilon<1\), the probability that the version space of \(Q\) (w.r.t. \(H\)) is not \(\epsilon\)-exhausted (w.r.t. \(c\)) is less than

\[|H|e^{-\epsilon m}.\]
theorems.proofHtml: Let \(h_1,\ldots,h_k\) be the hypotheses in \(H\) that have error greater than \(\epsilon\) with respect to \(c\). We fail to \(\epsilon\)-exhaust the version space w.r.t. \(H\) if and only if there is a hypothesis in this set that is consistent with all \(m\) independent random examples. Since each hypothesis has error greater than \(\epsilon\), an individual example of \(c\) is consistent with a given \(h_i\) with probability at most \((1-\epsilon)\). Thus, \(m\) independent random examples are consistent with \(h_i\) with probability at most \((1-\epsilon)^m\). Since the probability of a union of events is at most the sum of their individual probabilities, the probability that all \(m\) examples of \(c\) are consistent with any of the hypotheses in \(h_1,\ldots,h_k\) is at most \(k(1-\epsilon)^m\). The result follows from the fact that \(k\leq |H|\) and \((1-\epsilon)^m\leq e^{-\epsilon m}\) for \(0<\epsilon\leq1\) and \(m\geq0\).

theorems.title: Growth-function exhaustion bound
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.proofNote: The paper attributes this result to Blumer et al. and points also to Haussler and Welzl; it gives no proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(H\) is a hypothesis space and \(Q\) is a sequence of \(m\geq1\) independent random examples (chosen according to any fixed probability distribution on the instance space) of any target concept \(c\), then for any \(0<\epsilon<1\), the probability that the version space of \(Q\) (w.r.t. \(H\)) is not \(\epsilon\)-exhausted (w.r.t. \(c\)) is less than

\[2\Pi_H(2m)2^{-\epsilon m/2}.\]

theorems.title: Growth function from VC dimension
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.proofNote: The paper attributes this bound to Blumer et al., derived from Vapnik; it gives no proof.
theorems.statementHtml: If \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)=d\) and \(m\geq d\geq1\), then \(\Pi_H(m)\leq(em/d)^d\), where \(e\) is the base of the natural logarithm.

theorems.title: VC-dimension sample bound for exhaustion
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.proofNote: The immediately preceding paragraph says this follows by using Lemma 3.4 in Theorem 3.3 and cites the calculation in Blumer et al.; there is no separately labelled proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
If the sample \(Q\) has size at least

\[\bigl(4\log(2/\delta)+8\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\log(13/\epsilon)\bigr)/\epsilon,\]

then the version space of \(Q\) (w.r.t. \(H\)) is \(\epsilon\)-exhausted with probability at least \(1-\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml: As in the previous section, using Lemma 3.4 we can set the value given in Theorem 3.3 to \(\delta\) and “solve” for \(m\). From the calculation given in [5, Lemma A2.6] we have the following.

theorems.title: Growth and VC bounds for concept classes
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.proofNote: The proof is explicitly organized as Lemmas 3.7–3.11, with the final lower bounds cited to Littlestone.
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(X\) be an instance space defined by \(n\geq1\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear.

  1. If \(H\) is the hypothesis space of all pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\), then

    \[n\leq\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq2n\]

    and

    \[\Pi_H(m)\leq\left(\frac{em}{2n}\right)^{2n}\quad\text{for all }m\geq2n.\]

  2. If \(H\) is the hypothesis space of

    1. all pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\) that contain at most \(s\) atoms,
    2. all pure disjunctive concepts on \(X\) that contain at most \(s\) atoms, or
    3. all internal disjunctive concepts with at most \(s\) occurrences of tree-structured attribute values or linear attribute value ranges in all compound atoms combined,

    then

    \[\Pi_H(m)\leq n^s m^{2s}\quad\text{for all }m\geq2,\]

    \[\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq4s\log(4s\sqrt n),\]

    and

    \[s\lfloor\log(n/s)\rfloor\leq\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\quad\text{for }s\leq n.\]

  3. If \(H\) is the hypothesis space of all \(k\)-DNF concepts on \(X\) with at most \(s\) terms (or \(k\)-CNF concepts with at most \(s\) clauses), then

    \[\Pi_H(m)\leq n^{ks}m^{2ks}\quad\text{for all }m\geq2,\]

    \[\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq4ks\log(4ks\sqrt n),\]

    and

    \[ks\left\lfloor\log\frac{n}{ks^{1/k}}\right\rfloor\leq\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\quad\text{for }k\leq n\text{ and }s\leq\binom nk.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
The proof of this result is given in a series of lemmas.

The upper bound on \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\) in this case is established by Lemma 3.7 and Corollary 3.8. For the lower bound we will assume that the instance space \(X\) is defined by \(n\) Boolean attributes \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\). Since we are assuming throughout the paper that each attribute has at least two observable values, we can make this assumption without loss of generality. Let \(I\) be the set of instances

\[x_1=(\mathit{false},\mathit{true},\mathit{true},\ldots,\mathit{true}),\]
\[x_2=(\mathit{true},\mathit{false},\mathit{true},\ldots,\mathit{true}),\]
\[x_3=(\mathit{true},\mathit{true},\mathit{false},\ldots,\mathit{true}),\]
\[\vdots\]
\[x_n=(\mathit{true},\mathit{true},\mathit{true},\ldots,\mathit{false}).\]

It is easily verified that for any \(\{i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_k\}\subseteq\{1,\ldots,n\}\), the dichotomy of \(I\) in which all instances \(x_{i_1},x_{i_2},\ldots,x_{i_k}\) are labeled “−” and all others are labeled “+” is induced by the pure conjunctive concept

\[(A_{i_1}=\mathit{true})\text{ and }(A_{i_2}=\mathit{true})\text{ and }\cdots\text{ and }(A_{i_k}=\mathit{true}).\]

Hence, \(I\) is shattered by \(H\) and thus \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\geq n\).

Now note that by Lemma 3.4, if the VC dimension of \(H\) is \(2n\), then \(\Pi_H(m)\leq(\frac{1}{2}em/n)^{2n}\) for all \(m\geq2n\). This also holds for smaller VC dimensions, since for all \(k\), any hypothesis space with VC dimension less than \(k\) is contained in a hypothesis space with VC dimension equal to \(k\). This, combined with the above results, establishes Theorem 3.6(i).

The upper bounds in Theorem 3.6(ii) and (iii) follow directly from Lemmas 3.9–3.11. The lower bounds follow from [23, Lemma 4.6] (see also [23, Example 4 in Section 5]), which uses an example on an instance space of Boolean attributes remotely related to that given above for the lower bound in part (i). This completes the proof of Theorem 3.6.

theorems.title: VC upper bound for linear attributes
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.statementHtml: If \(X\) is an instance space defined by \(n\) linear attributes \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\), and \(H\) is the set of pure conjunctive concepts over \(X\), then \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq2n\).
theorems.proofHtml: Recall that instances in \(X\) are represented as \(n\)-tuples of values over \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\). Let \(I\) be a subset of \(X\) of cardinality \(2n+1\). For each \(i\), \(1\leq i\leq n\), choose a member of \(I\) that has the largest value for the attribute \(A_i\) among all members of \(I\), and a member of \(I\) that has the smallest value for the attribute \(A_i\) among all members of \(I\). Let \(S\) be the set of all members of \(I\) that are chosen. Elements of \(S\) will be called extreme members of \(I\). Clearly \(I\) can have at most \(2n\) extreme members, and thus \(I\) has at least one element that is not extreme. Furthermore, since the hypotheses of \(H\) are cross-products of intervals of values of the attributes \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\), it is easily verified that any hypothesis that includes all extreme members of \(I\) includes all members of \(I\). Hence, if we form a dichotomy of \(I\) by labeling all extreme members of \(I\) as positive instances and all other members of \(I\) as negative instances, then no hypothesis in \(H\) is consistent with this labeling. Thus \(I\) is not shattered by \(H\). It follows that no subset of cardinality \(2n+1\) can be shattered by \(H\), showing that the VC dimension of \(H\) is at most \(2n\).

theorems.title: VC upper bound for tree-structured or linear attributes
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.statementHtml: If \(X\) is an instance space defined by \(n\) attributes, each linear or tree-structured, and \(H\) is the set of pure conjunctive concepts over \(X\), then \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq2n\).
theorems.proofHtml: The observed values (leaves) of any tree-structured attribute can be ordered in such a way that any higher-level value (internal node) represents an interval of observed values. Hence, \(H\) is a subset of some \(H'\), where \(H'\) is the class of pure conjunctive concepts over some set of \(n\) linear attributes. Since the VC dimension of a subclass of concepts is never more than the VC dimension of the class itself, by the previous lemma, this implies that the VC dimension of \(H\) is at most \(2n\).

theorems.title: Growth bound for sparse conjunctive and disjunctive classes
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(H\) is the hypothesis space of

  1. all pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\) that contain at most \(s\) atoms,
  2. all pure disjunctive concepts on \(X\) that contain at most \(s\) atoms, or
  3. all internal disjunctive concepts with at most \(s\) occurrences of tree-structured attribute values or linear attribute value ranges in all compound atoms combined,

then

\[\Pi_H(m)\leq n^s m^{2s}\quad\text{for all }m\geq2.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(I\) be a set of \(m\geq2\) instances in \(X\). We first claim that for any (elementary) atom involving a linear attribute \(A\), there are at most \(\binom m2+m+1\) ways this atom can induce a dichotomy on the set \(I\) by partitioning it into positive instances whose values on \(A\) satisfy the atom and negative instances whose values do not. To see this, order the elements of \(I\) as \(x_1,\ldots,x_m\) such that for each \(i\), \(1\leq i<m\), the value of \(A\) on \(x_i\) is less than or equal to the value of \(A\) on \(x_{i+1}\). Since each atom involving the attribute \(A\) specifies an interval of values of \(A\), each such atom induces a dichotomy on \(I\) by making positive some interval of instances \(x_i,\ldots,x_j\), where \(1\leq i\leq j\leq m\), and making the rest negative, or by making all instances negative. This gives at most \(\binom m2+m+1\) dichotomies.

As in the previous lemma, since the leaves of any tree can be ordered so that the set of leaves of the subtree defined by any internal node forms an interval of this ordering, this result also holds for tree-structured attributes. (A tighter bound of at most \(2m\) dichotomies can also be derived for the tree-structured case.)

It is easily verified that \(\binom m2+m+1\leq m^2\) for all \(m\geq2\). Hence, we have shown that for each attribute \(A\), the atoms involving \(A\) are capable of inducing at most \(m^2\) dichotomies on a set \(I\) of \(m\) instances. The dichotomy induced by a hypothesis formed by the conjunction or disjunction of a set of atoms is entirely determined by the dichotomies induced by the individual atoms. Since it does not change the hypothesis to include the same atom more than once, we can assume without loss of generality that each hypothesis \(h\in H\) contains exactly \(s\) atoms. Since for each of the \(s\) atoms in the hypothesis \(h\) there are \(n\) ways to assign it an attribute and at most \(m^2\) ways to choose the dichotomy induced by its value range given its assigned attribute, this gives a bound of \((nm^2)^s=n^s m^{2s}\) on the total number of distinct dichotomies induced by \(H\) on \(I\). Hence, \(\Pi_H(m)\leq n^s m^{2s}\) in cases (a) and (b).

Clearly the same argument works in case (c) for internal disjunctive concepts. Once we have assigned attributes to elementary atoms, we can collect all the elementary atoms that share a common attribute together to form compound atoms and then form the conjunction of these. Every internal disjunctive concept can be formed in this way. The dichotomy it induces is determined by the dichotomies of the elementary atoms and the way attributes are assigned to them.

theorems.title: Growth bound for sparse k-DNF and k-CNF
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(H\) is the hypothesis space of all \(k\)-DNF concepts on \(X\) with at most \(s\) terms (or \(k\)-CNF concepts with at most \(s\) clauses), then

\[\Pi_H(m)\leq n^{ks}m^{2ks}\quad\text{for all }m\geq2.\]
theorems.proofHtml: By Lemma 3.9, the number of dichotomies induced by a single term of a \(k\)-DNF is at most \(n^k m^{2k}\). As above, we can assume that the \(k\)-DNF expression contains exactly \(s\) terms. Since the dichotomies induced by a \(k\)-DNF expression are determined by the dichotomies induced by each of its terms, there are at most \((n^k m^{2k})^s=n^{ks}m^{2ks}\) dichotomies induced by \(k\)-DNF expressions with \(s\) terms. Clearly the same argument works for \(k\)-CNF.

theorems.title: Polynomial growth becomes subexponential
theorems.section: 3. The Growth Function and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Dimension
theorems.statementHtml: Assume \(n,s\geq1\). Then for any \(m>4s\log(4s\sqrt n)\), \(n^s m^{2s}<2^m\).

theorems.title: Correctness of the classical conjunctive learner
theorems.section: 4. The Performance of the Classical Learning Algorithm for Conjunctive Concepts
theorems.statementHtml: If there exists a pure conjunctive concept consistent with the sample, Algorithm 4.1 will find the unique maximally specific such concept, otherwise it correctly reports that the sample is not consistent with any pure conjunctive concept.
theorems.proofHtml: Let \(h=a_1\) and \(a_2\) and \(\cdots\) and \(a_n\), where \(a_i\) is the minimal dominating atom for the attribute \(A_i\) w.r.t. \(Q\). For each \(i\), let \(V_i\) denote the set of values for \(A_i\) included in the atom \(a_i\). The hypothesis \(h\) represents the set of all instances in the cross-product of \(V_1,\ldots,V_n\). By the definition of a minimal dominating atom, for any positive example \((v_1,\ldots,v_n)\) we must have \(v_i\in V_i\) for all \(i\) and hence this example is included in \(h\). Thus if \(h\) does not include any negative examples, then it is consistent with \(Q\). On the other hand, since each \(a_i\) is the unique minimal dominating atom for \(A_i\), any other atom that includes all values of \(A_i\) that occur in positive examples must include all values in \(V_i\). Therefore any conjunction of such atoms must represent a hypothesis that includes all examples in the cross product of \(V_1,\ldots,V_n\). Therefore any pure conjunctive hypothesis that is consistent with the sample must contain \(h\). It follows that if \(h\) does not include any negative examples, then \(h\) is the unique maximally specific pure conjunctive hypothesis that is consistent with \(Q\), otherwise no pure conjunctive hypothesis is consistent with \(Q\).

theorems.title: Performance of consistent learning
theorems.section: 4. The Performance of the Classical Learning Algorithm for Conjunctive Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(H\) be a hypothesis space and \(L\) be a learning algorithm that uses \(H\) consistently. For any \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\), given

\[\bigl(4\log(2/\delta)+8\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\log(13/\epsilon)\bigr)/\epsilon\]

independent random examples of any target concept \(c\), with probability at least \(1-\delta\), algorithm \(L\) will either

  1. produce a hypothesis in \(H\) that has error at most \(\epsilon\) with respect to \(c\), or
  2. indicate correctly that the target concept \(c\) is not in \(H\).

Moreover, this result holds regardless of the particular probability distribution on the instance space that governs the generation of examples.
theorems.proofHtml: By Corollary 3.5, after this many examples the version space with respect to \(H\) is \(\epsilon\)-exhausted with probability \(1-\delta\). When the version space is \(\epsilon\)-exhausted then either it is empty, in which case, since \(L\) uses \(H\) consistently, \(L\) halts, indicating correctly that no hypothesis in \(H\) is consistent with the given examples of \(c\) and hence \(c\notin H\), or it is not empty, in which case \(L\) produces a hypothesis from this space and, because the space is \(\epsilon\)-exhausted, this hypothesis has error at most \(\epsilon\).

theorems.title: Classical conjunctive learner performance
theorems.section: 4. The Performance of the Classical Learning Algorithm for Conjunctive Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(X\) be an instance space defined by \(n\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear. For any \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\), given

\[\bigl(4\log(2/\delta)+16n\log(13/\epsilon)\bigr)/\epsilon\]

independent random examples of any target concept \(c\) defined on \(X\), with probability at least \(1-\delta\), Algorithm 4.1 will either

  1. produce a pure conjunctive hypothesis that has error at most \(\epsilon\) with respect to \(c\), or
  2. indicate correctly that the target concept \(c\) is not a pure conjunctive concept.

This holds for any probability distribution on \(X\) governing the generation of examples.
theorems.proofHtml: Lemma 4.2 shows that Algorithm 4.1 uses the hypothesis space \(H\) of pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\) consistently and Theorem 3.6(i) shows that \(\operatorname{VCdim}(H)\leq2n\). The result then follows directly from Theorem 4.4.

theorems.title: General sample-complexity lower bound
theorems.section: 4. The Performance of the Classical Learning Algorithm for Conjunctive Concepts
theorems.proofNote: The paper quotes this result from Ehrenfeucht et al. and gives no proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(C\) is a class of concepts with \(\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\geq2\), then there exists a positive constant \(c_0\) such that for all learning algorithms \(L\),

\[S_C^L(\epsilon,\delta)\geq c_0\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\bigr)/\epsilon\]

for all sufficiently small positive \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\).

theorems.title: Near-optimal sample complexity of Algorithm 4.1
theorems.section: 4. The Performance of the Classical Learning Algorithm for Conjunctive Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
There are positive constants \(c_0\) and \(c_1\) such that for any instance space \(X\) defined on \(n\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear,

\[c_0\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+n\bigr)/\epsilon\leq S_C^L(\epsilon,\delta)\leq c_1\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+n\log(1/\epsilon)\bigr)/\epsilon\]

for all sufficiently small \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\), where \(L\) is Algorithm 4.1 and \(C\) is the class of pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\). Moreover, this lower bound holds for any learning algorithm \(L\).
theorems.proofHtml: Using the fact that \(n\leq\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\) from Theorem 3.6(i), the first inequality follows from Theorem 4.7. The second inequality follows from Corollary 4.5.

theorems.title: Hardness of the smallest consistent conjunct
theorems.section: 5. Using a Greedy Heuristic to Improve Performance on Simpler Target Concepts
theorems.statementHtml: Given a sample on \(n\) attributes that is consistent with some pure conjunctive hypothesis, it is NP-hard to find a pure conjunctive hypothesis that is both consistent with this sample and has the minimum number of atoms.
theorems.proofHtml:
We will reduce the following problem, known to be NP-hard [13], to the above problem.

Minimum set cover problem. Given a collection of sets with union \(T\) (i.e. that cover \(T\)), find a subcollection whose union is \(T\) that has the minimum number of sets. This is called a minimum cover of \(T\).

Given an instance of the minimum set cover problem defined by the collection of sets \(S_1,\ldots,S_n\) with union \(T=\{x_1,\ldots,x_k\}\), let \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) be a set of Boolean attributes. Let the sample \(Q\) consist of one positive example \((\mathit{true},\mathit{true},\ldots,\mathit{true})\) followed by \(k\) negative examples \((v_{1,1},v_{1,2},\ldots,v_{1,n}),\ldots,(v_{k,1},v_{k,2},\ldots,v_{k,n})\), where for all \(i\), \(1\leq i\leq k\), and all \(j\), \(1\leq j\leq n\), \(v_{i,j}=\mathit{false}\) if \(x_i\in S_j\) and \(v_{i,j}=\mathit{true}\) otherwise.

Suppose that \(S_{i_1},\ldots,S_{i_p}\) is a subcollection of \(S_1,\ldots,S_n\) that covers \(T\). Then we claim that the hypothesis \(A_{i_1}=\mathit{true}\) and \(\cdots\) and \(A_{i_p}=\mathit{true}\) is consistent with \(Q\). To verify this, note that it clearly includes the positive example of \(Q\) and furthermore, because every \(x_i\), \(1\leq i\leq k\), appears in some \(S_{i_j}\), \(1\leq j\leq p\), every one of the negative examples has some attribute in \(A_{i_1},\ldots,A_{i_p}\) that is set to false, and thus is not included in this hypothesis.

On the other hand, if \(h\) is any pure conjunctive hypothesis that is consistent with \(Q\), then \(h\) must have the form \(A_{i_1}=\mathit{true}\) and \(\cdots\) and \(A_{i_p}=\mathit{true}\) for some \(\{i_1,\ldots,i_p\}\subseteq\{1,\ldots,n\}\), for otherwise it would not include the positive example of \(Q\). Furthermore, each of the negative examples of \(Q\) must have the value false for some attribute in \(A_{i_1},\ldots,A_{i_p}\), otherwise it would be included in \(h\). Because of the way the negative examples are defined, this implies that \(S_{i_1},\ldots,S_{i_p}\) cover \(T\).

It follows that finding the minimum cover of \(T\) from the given collection of sets reduces to finding the pure conjunctive hypothesis that is consistent with \(Q\) and has the minimum number of atoms. Hence, since the minimum set cover problem is NP-hard, so is the problem of finding the smallest consistent pure conjunctive hypothesis.

theorems.title: Greedy set-cover approximation
theorems.section: 5. Using a Greedy Heuristic to Improve Performance on Simpler Target Concepts
theorems.proofNote: The paper quotes this result from Johnson and Nigmatullin and gives no proof.
theorems.statementHtml: If the set \(T\) to be covered has \(m\) elements and \(s\) is the size of the minimum cover, then the greedy method is guaranteed to find a cover of size at most \(s(\ln m+1)\).

theorems.title: Effective-hypothesis-space error bound
theorems.section: 5. Using a Greedy Heuristic to Improve Performance on Simpler Target Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(C\) be a class of target concepts and let \(L\) be a learning algorithm that always produces a consistent hypothesis (not necessarily in \(C\)) when given a sample of a target concept in \(C\). Then given a sequence of \(m\geq1\) independent random examples (chosen according to any fixed probability distribution on the instance space) of any target concept \(c\in C\), for any \(0<\epsilon<1\), the probability that \(L\) returns a hypothesis with error greater than \(\epsilon\) is less than

\[2\Pi_{H_C^L(m)}(2m)2^{-\epsilon m/2}.\]
theorems.proofHtml: By Theorem 3.3, for any target concept \(c\) and distribution on the instance space, this is an upper bound on the probability that any hypothesis in \(H_C^L(m)\) with error greater than \(\epsilon\) is consistent with all \(m\) random examples of \(c\). Since \(L\) always produces a consistent hypothesis in \(H_C^L(m)\) for any sample of a target concept in \(C\), when the target concept is in \(C\) this is therefore an upper bound on the probability that the hypothesis returned by \(L\) has error greater than \(\epsilon\).

theorems.title: Solving a polynomial-logarithmic tail bound
theorems.section: 5. Using a Greedy Heuristic to Improve Performance on Simpler Target Concepts
theorems.proofNote: The paper says the result follows from Haussler [16, Lemma 1(iii)] and that calculations are outlined in its appendix.
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(f\) is a real-valued function and there exist \(a,b,d\geq1\) such that \(f(m)\leq a(bm)^{d\log m}\) for all \(m\geq2\), then there exists a constant \(c_1\) such that

\[f(m)2^{-\epsilon m/2}\leq\delta\]

for all \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\) and

\[m\geq c_1\bigl(\log(a/\delta)+d(\log(bd/\epsilon))^2\bigr)/\epsilon.\]
theorems.proofHtml: This follows from [16, Lemma 1(iii)]. The calculations are outlined in [16, Appendix].

theorems.title: Near-optimal sparse-conjunctive sample complexity
theorems.section: 5. Using a Greedy Heuristic to Improve Performance on Simpler Target Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
There are positive constants \(c_0\) and \(c_1\) such that for any instance space \(X\) defined on \(n\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear,

\[c_0\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+s\lfloor\log(n/s)\rfloor\bigr)/\epsilon\leq S_C^L(\epsilon,\delta)\leq c_1\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+s(\log(sn/\epsilon))^2\bigr)/\epsilon\]

for all sufficiently small \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\), where \(L\) is Algorithm 5.2 and \(C\) is the class of pure conjunctive concepts on \(X\) with at most \(s\) atoms, \(s\leq n\). Moreover, this lower bound holds for any learning algorithm \(L\).
theorems.proofHtml:
As in Corollary 4.8, the lower bound follows from Theorem 4.7, using the lower bound on \(\operatorname{VCdim}(C)\) given in Theorem 3.6(ii). For the upper bound, note that from Theorem 5.3 it follows that \(H_C^L(m)\) is contained in the class of pure conjunctive hypotheses with at most \(s(\ln m+1)\) atoms. Thus by Theorem 3.6(ii)

\[2\Pi_{H_C^L(m)}(2m)\leq2(2\sqrt n m)^{2s(\ln m+1)}\leq2(2\sqrt n m)^{4s\log m}\quad\text{for }m\geq2.\]

Now let \(a=2\), \(b=2\sqrt n\) and \(d=4s\). Then by Lemma 5.6 there exists a constant \(c_1\) such that

\[2\Pi_{H_C^L(m)}(2m)2^{-\epsilon m/2}\leq\delta\]

for all \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\) and \(m\geq c_1(\log(1/\delta)+s(\log(sn/\epsilon))^2)/\epsilon\). Hence, by Theorem 5.5, for any distribution on the instance space, given a random sample of this size of any target concept in \(C\), the probability that \(L\) produces a hypothesis with error greater than \(\epsilon\) is at most \(\delta\). Thus, this is an upper bound on the sample complexity of \(L\) for target concepts in \(C\).

theorems.title: Near-optimal k-DNF sample complexity
theorems.section: 6. Learning Pure Disjunctive, k-DNF and k-CNF Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
There are positive constants \(c_0\) and \(c_1\) such that for any instance space \(X\) defined on \(n\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear,

\[c_0\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+ks\lfloor\log(n/(ks^{1/k}))\rfloor\bigr)/\epsilon\leq S_C^L(\epsilon,\delta)\leq c_1\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+ks(\log(ksn/\epsilon))^2\bigr)/\epsilon\]

for all sufficiently small \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\), where \(L\) is the above algorithm and \(C\) is the class of all \(k\)-DNF concepts on \(X\) with at most \(s\) terms, \(k\leq n\) and \(s\leq\binom nk\). Moreover, this lower bound holds for any learning algorithm \(L\).
theorems.proofHtml: Similar to that of Corollary 5.7, but using Theorem 3.6(iii).

theorems.title: Near-optimal internal-disjunctive sample complexity
theorems.section: 7. Internal Disjunctive Concepts
theorems.statementHtml:
There are positive constants \(c_0\) and \(c_1\) such that for any instance space \(X\) defined on \(n\) attributes, each tree-structured or linear,

\[c_0\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+s\lfloor\log(n/s)\rfloor\bigr)/\epsilon\leq S_C^L(\epsilon,\delta)\leq c_1\bigl(\log(1/\delta)+s(\log(sn/\epsilon))^2\bigr)/\epsilon\]

for all sufficiently small \(\epsilon\) and \(\delta\), where \(L\) is Algorithm 7.1 and \(C\) is the class of internal disjunctive concepts on \(X\) with size at most \(s\), \(s\leq n\). Moreover, this lower bound holds for any learning algorithm \(L\).
